aiPersona:
You are an expert painting contractor assistant with deep knowledge of residential and commercial painting.
You understand surface preparation including sanding, priming, caulking, and patching before any paint is applied.
You know the difference between latex and oil-based paints and when each is appropriate.
You can recommend sheen levels (flat, eggshell, satin, semi-gloss, high-gloss) based on room type and surface.
You understand that exterior jobs require weather-appropriate scheduling and UV-resistant coatings.
You are familiar with cabinet refinishing workflows including degreasing, scuff-sanding, priming, and multi-coat spray or brush application.
You know that deck and fence staining involves pressure washing, drying time, and penetrating vs. film-forming stains.
You understand commercial painting requirements such as low-VOC regulations, after-hours scheduling, and large-scale spraying.
You always account for prep work in estimates because it typically represents 60-70% of a quality paint job.
You can calculate paint coverage based on square footage, number of coats, and surface porosity.
You recommend two coats minimum for color changes and one coat for refreshing existing colors.
You know that trim, doors, and ceilings are often priced separately from wall square footage.
You factor in the cost of painter's tape, drop cloths, and other consumables.
You understand that high ceilings, stairwells, and intricate trim add labor time and cost.
You communicate clearly with homeowners about dry times, ventilation, and when furniture can be moved back.
You always recommend premium paints for high-traffic areas and exteriors to ensure longevity.
You understand spray vs. brush vs. roller application and when each method is best for the job.
You know that proper masking and protection of floors, fixtures, and landscaping is essential.

defaultLineItems.description: Two coats of interior latex paint on walls, cut-in and roll
Flat finish ceiling paint, one to two coats as needed
Semi-gloss finish on baseboards, crown, and window trim
Paint interior door both sides including jamb and casing
Paint window frames, sills, and casings
Primer coat on new drywall or for major color changes
Fill nail holes, patch small dents, sand smooth before painting
Caulk gaps at trim joints, baseboards, and window casings for clean lines
Premium interior latex paint, color-matched, two coats coverage
Masking tape, drop cloths, plastic sheeting, roller covers, and brush set
Feature wall with dark or bold color requiring additional coats
Two coats exterior acrylic latex on siding, stucco, or wood
Paint fascia boards and soffit panels, ladder/scaffolding access
Window trim, corner boards, and other exterior trim elements
Pressure wash all surfaces to remove dirt, mildew, and loose paint
Hand scrape and sand peeling or flaking areas back to sound surface
Spot-prime or full-prime bare wood and repaired areas
Paint garage door face, typically one to two coats
Paint individual shutters, both sides, removed or in place
Paint front entry door including jamb and weather stripping mask-off
Caulk around windows, doors, trim joints, and penetrations
Premium exterior acrylic latex paint with UV and mildew resistance
Sand, prime, and spray two coats on each cabinet door
Sand, prime, and spray two coats on each drawer front
Paint exposed cabinet frames and interior edges where visible
TSP wash and degrease all cabinet surfaces before sanding
120-180 grit scuff sand for adhesion on all surfaces
Spray bonding primer coat for maximum adhesion to slick surfaces
Remove hinges, pulls, and knobs before painting and reinstall after cure
Cabinet-grade enamel paint, bonding primer, sandpaper, and supplies
Apply two coats of penetrating or film-forming deck stain
Spray and back-brush stain on both sides of fence
Pressure wash deck to remove dirt, mildew, and old stain
Pressure wash fence panels and posts
Apply wood brightener/neutralizer after washing for even stain absorption
Stain deck railings, balusters, and post caps
Exterior deck/fence stain, penetrating or semi-transparent
Replace cracked or split boards, re-nail loose boards
Large-area wall painting using airless spray and back-roll method
Spray flat ceiling paint in open office or retail space
Paint corridors and common areas, often requires durable finishes
Full paint of individual office suite including walls, trim, and doors
Semi-gloss moisture-resistant paint for commercial restrooms
Two-part epoxy floor system for warehouse, shop, or garage areas
Premium rate for work performed evenings/weekends to avoid business disruption
Low-VOC commercial-grade interior paint, color matched
Upgrade to zero-VOC paint for LEED or sensitive environments

terminologyGlossary.sheen: The level of shine in a paint finish, ranging from flat to high-gloss
terminologyGlossary.cutting in: Painting a straight edge along trim, ceiling lines, or corners using a brush before rolling
terminologyGlossary.mil thickness: Measurement of paint film thickness; one mil equals one-thousandth of an inch
terminologyGlossary.VOC: Volatile Organic Compounds - chemicals in paint that evaporate and affect air quality
terminologyGlossary.primer: A preparatory coating applied before paint to improve adhesion and coverage
terminologyGlossary.latex paint: Water-based paint that is easy to clean up and dries quickly
terminologyGlossary.oil-based paint: Alkyd paint that provides a harder, more durable finish but requires mineral spirits for cleanup
terminologyGlossary.eggshell: A slight sheen finish between flat and satin, popular for living rooms and bedrooms
terminologyGlossary.semi-gloss: A moderately shiny finish ideal for trim, doors, kitchens, and bathrooms
terminologyGlossary.back-rolling: Rolling over a sprayed surface immediately to ensure even coverage and adhesion
terminologyGlossary.flashing: Uneven sheen on a painted surface caused by inconsistent application or surface porosity
terminologyGlossary.feathering: Blending the edges of a paint repair to match the surrounding area seamlessly
terminologyGlossary.tack cloth: A sticky cloth used to remove dust from surfaces before painting
terminologyGlossary.bonding primer: A specialty primer designed to adhere to slick surfaces like cabinets or laminate
terminologyGlossary.dry time vs cure time: Dry time is when paint is dry to touch; cure time is when it reaches full hardness (usually 30 days)

seasonalTips.tip: Ideal time to start exterior painting projects once temperatures consistently stay above 50°F. Book early as spring is peak season.
Avoid painting exterior surfaces in direct sunlight or extreme heat above 90°F as paint can dry too quickly and blister. Early morning starts are best.
Last chance for exterior projects before temperatures drop. Ensure paint has time to cure before overnight lows hit the 30s.
Focus on interior painting projects. Ensure adequate ventilation and humidity control for proper drying. Great time for cabinet refinishing.

followUpToneNotes: Painting customers often care about color selection and aesthetics. Be warm and consultative. Emphasize prep work quality and the longevity of a professional finish versus DIY. Offer color consultation as a value-add. Follow up with photos of completed work when possible.
